text: In 2009, Paul Graham identified a structural incompatibility at the heart of creative organizations: the maker's schedule and the manager's schedule operate on fundamentally different units of time. Managers think in hours — a meeting at 11 means the morning is sliced into two fragments. Makers think in half-days or full days. A single meeting dropped into the middle of an afternoon doesn't cost one hour; it destroys the entire block, because deep creative work requires a long runway before it reaches altitude. The cost is asymmetric, invisible on a calendar, and almost never factored into how organizations allocate time.
question: According to the passage, why does a single meeting cost a maker more than one hour of lost time?

options.text: Makers take longer to commute to meeting rooms than managers do.
Meetings introduce social anxiety that lingers for hours afterward.
Deep creative work requires a long uninterrupted runway to reach productive altitude.
Managers deliberately schedule meetings during peak creative hours.
